# Questions in Livestream 242

Live Stream #242: Happy New Year 2026

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=612" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:12</a> | How can you tell what language a cuneiform text is written in? | He said the real way is to read it and recognize the language from its forms and spelling habits. | He explained that cuneiform is a writing system used for several languages, including Akkadian, Sumerian, Hittite, Hurrian, Ugaritic, and Amorite. The practical test is whether, after studying a language, the reader recognizes the words and forms; if not, the text is probably in another cuneiform language. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=704" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:44</a> | Will your upcoming Exodus book be available as an ebook? | Probably, if the publisher accepts it. He said ebook publication would be the normal expectation. | He stressed that the book had not yet been accepted by the publisher, but if it is, the publisher will control formats and will probably issue an ebook because that is normal. He added that image-heavy nonfiction loses value in ebook form because photographs are downgraded, unlike novels where ebook format works well. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=841" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:01</a> | Did Akkadian and related cuneiform spellings lose some final consonant distinctions? | Yes. He said the spelling system can flatten distinctions and become inconsistent in ways that reflect the script and language history. | He said Akkadian spelling can be inconsistent, with cuneiform signs and syllables producing many spelling variations. In practice, readers often identify the word from the first few signs, while endings may represent conjugation or consonantal complements, a pattern he described as typical for cuneiform Semitic language work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=912" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:12</a> | Would wet sifting destroy small clay tablets or unfired pottery? | Not usually. He said the process is controlled and normally does not ruin such finds. | He described wet sifting as a brief spray in a trough, not soaking artifacts for a long time. The water removes surface dust and changes contrast so small items can be found; it can expose unfired items without meaningfully damaging them, though leaving them underwater overnight would be harmful. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=1026" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:06</a> | What happened to some of the old big supporters on the channel? | He said he could not speak with certainty about individual people, though one donor left over a disagreement about the channel's broader cultural mission. | He avoided claiming knowledge about every supporter, but said one case involved a content dispute over the channel discussing his books and broader cultural engagement. He framed the channel's mission as wider than Egypt, Bible teaching, and apologetics alone, including Christian art, cultural influence, and raising future leaders, and said donors support the mission rather than reshaping it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=1401" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:21</a> | If there are only a few generations from Nahshon to David, how does that fit any Exodus date? | He said biblical genealogies can telescope, so a short listed sequence does not require short generation lengths. | He said small genealogies can be abbreviated, so five named generations should not decide between Exodus chronologies. He also argued that roughly 200 years from conquest to David divided across five generations yields about forty years per generation, which he did not consider unrealistic, comparing it with his own family genealogy across about 250 years. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=1528" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:28</a> | You mentioned releasing an audiobook that did poorly. Where was it released? | He said it was distributed through Spotify and other stores, but not Audible because Audible would not accept the normal royalty terms. | He said the audiobook was available through Spotify, Google Play, and many other audiobook stores, but Audible wanted terms that would reduce the royalty drastically. He compared normal audiobook income of about six dollars to an Audible-credit payout he described as about fifteen cents, so he preferred not to place it on Audible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=1834" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:34</a> | How are Logos royalties when someone buys your book there? | He said Logos royalties are relatively good, around a quarter of the sale, better than many other book formats. | He said Logos pays about twenty-five percent, which he described as among the best book royalties he receives. By contrast, he said hardcovers and softcovers through Amazon bring about two dollars, and discounted ebooks bring much less, though he values ebook sales for reach. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=1926" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:06</a> | If the Persians invented crucifixion, when did they do so and who was the first victim? | He said they likely introduced it early on, but we do not know the first victim. | He said the first victim is unknown, but Persian crucifixion appears early in external reports such as Herodotus. He described the Persian form as closer to impaling than the later Roman cross, and he connected that kind of execution to the story of Haman. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=2056" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:16</a> | Was there a lot of crucifying when the Persians came back to power in Egypt in 343 BC? | Yes. He said the Persian return to power in Egypt became very ugly. | He answered briefly but directly that there was a lot of crucifying when Persia regained Egypt. He characterized that return to power as especially ugly and violent. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=2091" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:51</a> | When the Bible says God is slow to anger, does the Hebrew literally mean he has a long nose? | Yes. He said that idiom is real and underlies the usual translation. | He pointed to Exodus 34:6 and explained that the Hebrew idiom uses "long" with "nose" or "face" because anger was associated with the nose. A short nose means a short fuse, while a long nose means a long fuse, so the idiom naturally conveys patience or being slow to anger. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=2274" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:54</a> | Does John 12:31 imply Satan has some sort of authority as ruler of this age? | Yes in a limited sense. He said Satan has real power in the fallen world, though not lawful sovereignty. | He distinguished power from rightful authority, saying John uses language like "ruler" for a de facto ruler rather than a lawful king. His analogy was that Satan is a squatter in God's house: he exercises real power in the fallen world, but not a legitimate lease or ownership. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=2509" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:49</a> | In ancient Egypt, if someone committed a crime against me, would I go to a nomarch, judge, pharaoh, or priest? | He said ordinary cases would typically go through judges, often priests, but enforcement could be messy and serious class-crossing crimes could reach Pharaoh. | He said Egypt had judges, often priests, but not law codes in the modern sense, so enforcement could be inconsistent and vigilante justice or riots could occur locally. Serious cases that crossed class boundaries, such as a slave accused against a noblewoman, could be remanded to Pharaoh, while ordinary civil disputes might drag on even after repeated favorable judgments. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=2846" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:26</a> | Were the primordial gods under the waters when Ra's egg emerged? | Yes. He said they belonged to the primordial waters that existed before Ra emerged. | He answered that the primordial gods were under or within the primordial waters when Ra emerged. He treated the question as part of Egyptian cosmogonic imagery rather than developing a long explanation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=2892" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:12</a> | Can you give an in-depth rebuttal of the sermon about different Greek words for love in John? | He rejected the common sermon distinction between eros, phileo, and agapao, saying those tidy categories do not reflect how Greek usage works. | He said the neat sermon categories of eros as erotic love, phileo as brotherly love, and agapao as divine love are not how Greek usage works. He explained that phileo and agapao overlapped heavily, that agapao could even be used for loving sin, and that eros could be used in non-erotic phrases such as love of philosophy, making the tidy pulpit distinction an exegetical fallacy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=3218" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:38</a> | Is Michael a prince in Daniel or an archangel in Jude? | He said Michael is an archangel, while prince is a lower descriptive title used in Daniel. | He said Michael is technically an archangel, a job title meaning a high or trusted messenger rather than a species of spirit being. "Prince" translates a lower title, and he suggested Michael was probably a cherub in kind, though the exact hierarchy remains uncertain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=3444" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:24</a> | Do you have any thoughts on Christian Zionism? | He said he does not reject its basic premise, but he cautioned Christians to be wise about which causes they support. | He defined Christian Zionism as seeing the modern state of Israel as heir to Abrahamic promises and said he is not a supersessionist, so he has no objection to the basic religious premise. His criticism was practical: Christians can support Israel unwisely, including funding groups he sees as counter-gospel, and should remember Messianic Jews and Arab Christians in Israel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=3682" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:01:22</a> | Do animals know God exists, and do they have a relationship with him in this life? | He said Scripture shows a covenant relationship between God and animals; whether animals consciously know God exists is uncertain. | He cited Genesis 9 as establishing a covenant between God and living creatures, so animals have a relationship with God because God knows them and binds himself toward them. He was unsure whether animals consciously know God exists, but said animal mourning or funerary behavior may suggest some awareness of death, ritual, or something beyond ordinary life. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=3982" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:06:22</a> | What do you think of Bietak's multiple-exoduses theory? | He said Bietak is a strong excavator but has problems tying evidence from different sites into a coherent Exodus history. | He praised Bietak as a great excavator but criticized his ability to synthesize evidence across sites into a coherent history. He also said Bietak's Exodus views have changed repeatedly over the years, making his current multiple-exoduses theory difficult to pin down and leaving holes in the proposal. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=4104" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:24</a> | What is the point of the Song of the Sea for minimalists, and do any allow a natural sea miracle? | He said minimalists struggle with the Song of the Sea because its Hebrew looks archaic and early. | He said minimalists dislike the Song of the Sea because its Hebrew appears very archaic and, in his view, late Bronze Age or very early. He noted that some try to call it fake archaic language or late-date it, but he sees it as one of the oldest parts of the Pentateuch and problematic for minimalist reconstructions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=4389" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:09</a> | How can someone recognize a genuine miracle and distinguish it from exaggerated Hollywood-style claims? | He said that starts with defining miracle carefully and distinguishing first-order, second-order, and personal miracles. | He divided miracles into first-order violations of nature, second-order miracles of timing within nature, and third-order personal miracles. He said recognition depends on the particulars: a first-order miracle must truly violate natural law, while a second-order miracle may be a natural event at the right time, and possible natural explanations should be scrutinized before making a claim. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=4698" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:18:18</a> | Are prince and archangel the same thing for Michael, or do both titles just apply to him? | He said the titles both apply to Michael but are not the same thing: archangel is a job title and prince is a royal or command title. | He said "archangel" describes Michael's messenger office, while "prince" is a royal or command title that applies to him as a commander of the heavenly host. He compared Michael to field command rather than treating the terms as identical labels. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=4809" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:09</a> | What is the difference between a prince and a chief prince? | He said the difference is basically rank: a chief prince is a top prince. | He treated the distinction as a matter of hierarchy rather than a different kind of being or role. A chief prince is simply a higher-ranking or top prince. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=4862" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:02</a> | In Genesis 22:8, can "for himself" be translated as "as himself"? | No. He said the Hebrew uses a lamed preposition, so "for himself" is the better reading, not "as himself." | He checked the Hebrew and said the form uses the lamed preposition, meaning to, for, belonging to, or regarding. If the sense were "as" or "like," he would expect a different preposition, so the context supports "for himself" rather than "as himself." |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=4957" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:37</a> | Is there significance to the Genesis 22 ram being a ram? | Yes, but not a strong symbolic point. He said it is a ritually pure animal suitable for sacrifice. | He said the ram matters because it is a clean, ritually pure animal and acceptable for sacrifice, though he did not see a strong special symbolism. He added that patriarchal sacrifice predates Sinai's rules and was broader, but the narrative still presents the ram in terms of cleanness and ritual suitability. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=5193" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:26:33</a> | Why do some papers write ancient Near Eastern words with mixed upper- and lower-case letters? | He said those spellings often signal logograms or ideograms in cuneiform notation. | He explained that small capitals usually mark a logogram or ideogram, often functioning somewhat like a determinative while still being pronounced in cuneiform languages. He gave divine-name notation as an example and said these conventions are common in Akkadian, Sumerian, and Hittite, but not Ugaritic in the same way because it is alphabetic. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=5351" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:11</a> | When would you date Judges 4 relative to Egypt? | He dated it around the end of Dynasty 20 or beginning of Dynasty 21, roughly around 1100 BC. | He said both early and late Exodus chronologies often arrive around the same 1100 BC window for Judges 4, though by different methods. Early daters tend to stack judges in succession, while late daters divide the period differently, and he saw no reason to reject the broad consensus. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=5445" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:45</a> | Is a horse bone in a Thirteenth Dynasty foundation deposit evidence for chariots that early? | No. He said a horse bone alone does not prove chariot use and more likely reflects a food or offering context. | He said native Egyptian horses existed but were not suitable for riding or chariots, and chariot-suitable horses came later through Syrian and Levantine imports and breeding. A leg bone in a foundation deposit does not show bit or bridle wear; he would need jawbone evidence, and he treated the find as likely a meat or offering deposit rather than chariot evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=5874" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:54</a> | What do you make of the claim that a Merneptah place-name should be identified with Asher? | He rejected that identification as very weak. | He said the proposed identification does not make sense linguistically because the Egyptian place-name does not correspond well to Asher's consonants. He also criticized the proponent's Egyptian-language competence and regarded the claim as another example of weak cross-disciplinary overreach. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=6028" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:28</a> | What role do the ophanim, the wheels within wheels in Ezekiel, fill? | He said they are living spirit beings associated with moving and supporting God's throne, not UFOs or machinery. | After reading Ezekiel's wheel vision, he said the text presents the wheels as living beings with spirit in them. He connected the imagery to throne movement in ancient Near Eastern royal and divine iconography: the ophanim function below the throne, carrying or moving God's throne while cherubim and seraphim occupy other parts of the holy space. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=6550" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:10</a> | Are there good books or Christian documentaries for dismissing ancient-aliens or UFO claims? | He mentioned Clifford Wilson as an older source but said he does not keep up with that literature enough to recommend much else. | He said Clifford Wilson wrote older material on UFO and ancient-aliens claims, but Wilson has been dead for many years. He did not claim current expertise in that literature and was unsure whether Heiser wrote directly on it, so his recommendation was limited. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=6669" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:51:09</a> | Was Nehemiah related to Ezra or Esther? | He said he was not aware of a blood relation and the opening of Nehemiah does not clearly show one. | He checked Nehemiah 1 and noted that it names Nehemiah as son of Hacaliah and mentions his brother and men from Judah, but nothing that clearly links him by blood to Ezra or Esther. He left open that some relationship could exist but said he had not seen evidence for it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=6796" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:16</a> | In Deuteronomy 34:5, could a key phrase be translated "by" instead of "according to"? | He checked the text and did not treat "by" as persuasive; "according to" fits the context better. | He said the Hebrew construction points to senses like up to, until, or to the extent of, which can yield "according to" in context. He rejected "by" as an unhelpful translation here and emphasized that context supports the usual rendering. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=7000" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:56:40</a> | How do ransom theory and Christus Victor differ? | He said they are nearly the same idea under different labels, though he also raised theological problems with ransom theory. | He summarized ransom theory as Satan holding humanity hostage or holding the deed to mankind, with Christ's death paying the devil and becoming victory over him. He objected that this implies Satan has valid authority or ownership, whereas he sees Satan more as a squatter and says the debt for sin is owed to God, not Satan. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=7272" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:01:12</a> | Did Satan agree to sell the world back to Christ in ransom theory? | He said that contractual arrangement is not in Scripture and is a theological inference. | He said Scripture does not present a contract in which Satan agrees to sell the world back to Christ. The idea is inferred by theologians from ransom-theory logic, but he argued that the biblical debt is against God's holiness and that Satan simply moved into the profane space after God withdrew because of sin. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=7425" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:03:45</a> | What is Satan doing right now? | He said Satan knows he is defeated and is delaying the inevitable as long as he can. | He said Satan is trying to delay the conversion of the full number of Gentiles because, once that number is reached, the clock starts again and the end comes quickly. He compared Satan to a condemned person filing appeal after appeal to postpone an already certain sentence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=7530" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:05:30</a> | Does the clock stop ticking if Gentiles fall away from the faith? | He said yes, but God knows how many will come to faith and the clock resumes when that number is reached. | He said if Gentiles fall away before the full number comes in, the clock effectively stops while God waits for the remaining Gentiles who will come to faith. Once that number is reached, the clock resumes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=7607" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:06:47</a> | Are people reborn on earth until they find Christ? | No. He said Christianity does not teach reincarnation or transmigration; people die once and then face judgment. | He rejected reincarnation and transmigration as non-Christian ideas. He cited the Christian teaching that people are appointed once to die and then judgment, so earthly life is a single opportunity rather than repeated lives. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=7692" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:08:12</a> | How should we assess arguments for single authorship versus multi-stage composition of Isaiah? | He favors single authorship, especially because he sees Isaiah as a large chiastic or reflective composition. | He acknowledged that single authorship is a minority view, but said he finds Isaiah's large reflective or chiastic structure compelling, including an inflection point associated with the Great Isaiah Scroll. He argued that multi-stage theories often depend on naturalistic assumptions about prophecy, especially around Cyrus, and he does not think multiple Isaiahs are necessary. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=7995" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:13:15</a> | Is a generation always thirty years? | No. He said generation length varies too much to lock it to one fixed number. | He said attempts to fix a generation at 25, 30, 35, 40, 60, or 100 years all fail because father-to-child intervals vary widely. He also said some biblical uses of "generation" may refer to an ethos or way of thinking rather than a fixed biological timespan, so the preterist use he was addressing fails on context as well. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=8191" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:16:31</a> | What is the current consensus on pyramid construction techniques, and have ideas changed recently? | He said evidence for ports and ramp or pulley systems has refined how scholars think large stones were transported and raised. | He said recent discoveries changed parts of the picture: a port near the pyramids suggests stones could be floated in, and evidence from Hatnub supports moving stones up steep inclines using ramps, ropes, stakes, and wooden pulleys. Ramps were already expected, but the stake-and-pulley leverage system refined the mechanism. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=8431" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:20:31</a> | Do you know whether Inspiring Philosophy is going to do a series on Daniel? | He said he does not know Inspiring Philosophy's plans, though Rob had announced something on Daniel. | He said Inspiring Philosophy does not run plans by him, so he does not know whether that channel will do Daniel. He distinguished that from Rob, whom he said had announced something on Daniel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=8479" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:21:19</a> | How were artisans and scribes paid in the ancient Near East? | He said Egyptians were usually paid in rations and goods rather than money in the modern sense. | He said Egyptian workers were typically paid in food and goods, such as barley, wheat, wine, oil, dried fish, dried meat, and medical supplies. Side work could be paid in items such as sandals, cloth, chests, livestock, or other goods, with value depending on what was traded. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=8963" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:29:23</a> | Is John 6:48-58 about the Eucharist? | He said no: in his reading, the passage is about the hard words of Christ and discipleship, not Eucharistic ritual. | He said John 6 does not mention the Lord's Supper and should not be read as Eucharistic ritual in that context. Instead, he read Jesus' flesh-and-blood language as intentionally difficult teaching that winnowed followers, forced disciples to accept hard words, and prepared them for sacrificial discipleship. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=9631" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:40:31</a> | If the Persians invented crucifixion, who invented typical cross crucifixion? | He said the Romans developed the typical cross form, though crucifixion existed before Rome. | He distinguished Persian impaling-style crucifixion from the later Roman cross form. The Romans developed the familiar structure with an upright stake and a crossbeam, even though the broader category of crucifixion predated them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=9685" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:41:25</a> | How do you reconcile Adam and Eve with evolution? | He said he does not try to reconcile them because Genesis 1-11 is a ritual and theological text, not a scientific origins account. | He said Genesis 1-11 functions as a religious prolegomenon establishing temple or tabernacle ritual, so attempts to harmonize it with scientific origin models miss the point. He rejected concordist approaches, including creationist and theistic-evolutionary harmonizations, because he thinks they do violence to the text's original meaning. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=9833" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:43:53</a> | What is your opinion on the star the wise men followed to find Jesus? | He said it was likely a normal conjunction or omen-like sign rather than a supernatural moving star or spectacular astronomical event. | He said the sign was probably a normal conjunction or planetary-star alignment interpreted through omen logic like ancient Assyrian omen lists. He did not picture a spectacular bright object moving through the sky, but a meaningful astronomical sign that the wise men knew how to read. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=9986" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:46:26</a> | Why have different Greek words for love if they mean the same thing? | He said language history creates synonyms and overlapping meanings; words can share meaning while carrying different histories or usage pressures. | He explained that languages accumulate synonyms through history, contact, divergence, and shifts in usage, as English did with Anglo-Saxon and French vocabulary. For phileo and agapao, he said confusion with a similar-sounding word for kissing helped agapao take over some uses, so overlap does not mean the words originated or functioned identically in every context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=10569" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:56:09</a> | Is "snitch" synonymous with "purloin"? | He said it is probably closer to purloin than to snatch because snitch can mean stealing privately. | He accepted that "snitch" can be close to "purloin" because both can involve private or stealthy taking. He contrasted this with "snatch," which emphasizes quick taking, and used the example of quietly stealing cookies rather than grabbing them fast. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=10721" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:58:41</a> | Are we getting to a point where words mean nothing? | He said the problem is more that words are being flattened toward one meaning, causing loss of nuance. | He said words are not becoming meaningless so much as readers are losing semantic range and moving toward one-word, one-meaning assumptions. He saw that as a loss for language and writing style because nuanced distinctions get stripped away. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=10805" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:00:05</a> | How is "snitch" as leaking information related to "snitch" as stealing privately? | He said he did not know the exact history and only speculated that secrecy may be the common link. | He treated this as an etymological question he could not answer on the spot. His only tentative suggestion was that secrecy might connect the two senses, but he clearly marked that as speculation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=10845" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:00:45</a> | Is kairos "deep time" in an esoteric sense? | He rejected that framing and described kairos as meaningful or significant time. | He said kairos is better understood as meaningful or significant time, not an esoteric layer of "deep time." He contrasted it with another Greek time term tied more to occasion or the right thing happening at the right time, emphasizing nuance rather than mystical speculation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=11017" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:03:37</a> | Does aionios mean an age? | He said an aion is an age or vast period, and discussed the Greek phrase often translated "forever and ever." | He said an aion is an unspecified vast period of time and highlighted the Greek phrase "ages of ages" as a powerful recursive expression often translated "forever and ever." He connected that phrasing to his rejection of annihilationism, saying any annihilation would not be soon in that framework. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=11160" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:06:00</a> | Why did Egyptians often use limestone for stelae instead of sandstone? | He said limestone carves better, holds detail and paint better, and is more suitable for durable inscriptions. | He said fine limestone preserves detail much better than sandstone, while sandstone carvings can look poor and erode badly. Limestone was especially good for painted family dedication stelae, while wealthier or royal stelae might use harder stones such as granite. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=11726" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:15:26</a> | Is the reported discovery of the top half of a Ramesses II statue real or just rumor? | Real, though he said it is not as sensational as some people make it sound. | He said the top half of a Ramesses II statue had indeed been found and was verified, not just rumored. He tempered the significance by noting that many Ramesses II statues exist, so it is real but not necessarily earthshattering. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=11764" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:16:04</a> | Would an obsidian blade in Dungeons and Dragons be legitimate? | Yes. He said obsidian blades existed, but they were brittle and generally limited in size. | He said obsidian blades are historically legitimate because people did make them, but they are brittle and cannot be made very large. He estimated the largest practical blades at about eight inches, so he thought dagger rather than large sword. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=11939" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:18:59</a> | What do you think of cases where stage-four cancers disappear? | He said those could be personal or third-order miracles, but cancers can also go into spontaneous remission. | He classified such cases as possible third-order or personal miracles, but warned that they are not automatically proof of divine intervention. Since cancers can sometimes go into spontaneous remission and bodies can fight back, each case has to be evaluated with that possibility in mind. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=12351" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:25:51</a> | What category of miracle was Jesus healing the severed ear? | He classified that as a first-order miracle. | He said the instantaneous healing of an open wound and severed ear violates the normal laws of nature. Because of that direct violation, he placed it in the first-order miracle category. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=12433" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:27:13</a> | Why was sandstone not more popular for Egyptian stelae? | He said sandstone was poor material for stelae because it does not hold carving, paint, or water exposure well. | He said stelae were meant to preserve text and imagery for posterity, and sandstone does that badly. It absorbs paint, loses color, holds carving poorly, and can crumble or shed inscriptions when water-soaked, whereas limestone preserves painted and carved detail better. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=12702" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:31:42</a> | Is there a record of a god dying of hunger? | He said not in ancient Egypt, though Mesopotamian gods could be depicted as starving or fearful. | He said Egyptian gods are fed through offerings, but not depicted as starving to death in the way some Mesopotamian traditions portray gods huddled or fearful without offerings. He explained Egyptian offerings as reciprocal economy: one gives bread, wine, or gold to the god and expects return abundance. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=12886" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:34:46</a> | How are your War of the Gods books Christian art? | He said they are Christian because they use Christian themes and a biblical supernatural framework. | He said the books are Christian art because they look at the world through a Christian lens, using Christian themes such as prayer and a supernatural framework drawn from biblical material such as Jude. He stressed that this does not mean preaching conversion scenes at readers, but embedding Christian worldview and themes into the story. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=13016" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:36:56</a> | Did that prayer include requests for the downfall of the scumbag youth pastor character? | No. He said that was not part of it. | He said the prayer in the story does not include requests for that character's downfall. He clarified that the referenced character is not a youth pastor, though the character was based partly on a real case study involving something a youth pastor had done. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=13206" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:40:06</a> | Should someone switch from environmental science and zoology to paleontology if that is what they now want to do? | He said to pursue the field they love, find schools that actually offer paleontology, apply early, and look for financial aid. | He encouraged pursuing paleontology if that is the real interest, even though it is likely more expensive and requires schools with the proper labs and fossil collections. He advised applying early to programs that offer it, filing separate admissions and financial-aid applications, looking for bursaries, scholarships, grants, and even oil-company funding because paleontologists can be useful in oil prospecting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=14573" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:02:53</a> | Any suggestions for learning Unix? | He recommended getting a spare PC or small system, installing a beginner-friendly Linux distribution, and learning by using it directly. | He recommended installing Ubuntu or a beginner-friendly Linux distribution on a separate spare system, not on the primary work computer. He warned beginners away from dual boot or virtualized setups because bootloader or configuration mistakes can break the main system, and suggested a cheap small PC or NUC as a safe learning machine. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=15078" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:11:18</a> | Have you ever been asked to help curate a museum exhibit? | Yes, but only in connection with student artwork rather than a major museum project. | He answered that he had been asked to help curate an exhibit, but only for student artwork. He did not claim experience curating a major museum or antiquities exhibit. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=15134" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:12:14</a> | What is your stance on 2 Maccabees saying the Ark of the Covenant was hidden? | He said he does not treat 2 Maccabees as reliable history for the Ark and puts little stock in that account. | He dated 2 Maccabees late and said he does not regard it as Scripture or reliable history for the Ark. He contrasted it with Jeremiah's statement that the Ark would not come to mind again, so he treated the hidden-Ark account as myth put to pen rather than dependable evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=15260" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:14:20</a> | Did Jesus and the apostles preach publicly in hostile environments? | Yes. He said the apostles preached in very hostile settings and sometimes faced riots, beatings, and death. | He said the apostles preached in environments hostile enough to involve beatings, being thrown out of town, riots, stonings, and martyrdom. He used Paul and James as examples and emphasized that being called by God does not mean the work will be easy or safe. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/PnupeC8FKmI?t=15387" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:16:27</a> | Should Christians bother preaching to atheists in the West, or do they already have no excuse? | He said it depends on the kind of atheist and thought deconverted atheists are often the least promising audience. | He distinguished between atheists who left Christianity and atheists who never were Christian. In his view, deconverted or deconstructed atheists are usually a long shot because they have already rejected the faith, while people raised atheist and never formally rejecting Christianity may be a more fruitful audience. |
